skillName: Mental strategies for multiplication and division
misconceptionSummary: Students use long multiplication mentally for every calculation, losing track of partial products.

lines.text: Regroup to create a round number

steps.narration: Factor pairs unlock round numbers. Sixteen is four times four; four times twenty-five is one hundred. So sixteen times twenty-five equals four times one hundred — four hundred. One rearrangement, no long multiplication.

lines.text: Product is unchanged — simpler to calculate

steps.narration: Halve one factor and double the other — the product stays the same. Twenty-four times five becomes twelve times ten, which is instantly one hundred and twenty.

lines.text: Divide in stages using factor pairs

steps.narration: Split the divisor into its factors and divide in stages. Eight hundred and forty divided by twenty-four: divide by four first to get two hundred and ten, then divide by six to get thirty-five.
Thirty-five. Splitting into factor steps keeps each division simple — no long division needed.

misconceptionStrip.text: Common mistake: using long multiplication or long division mentally for numbers like 16 × 25. Look for factor pairs that create round numbers — 16 × 25 = 4 × 4 × 25 = 4 × 100.
misconceptionStrip.audioNarration: Before calculating, look for shortcuts. Factor pairs, doubling and halving, and dividing in stages all turn difficult calculations into trivial ones.
